coredata.dc:title: Tryptophan capped gold–silver bimetallic nanoclusters-based turn-off fluorescence sensor for the determination of histamine
coredata.dc:creator: Swathy, S.
Pallam, Goldamol S.
Girish Kumar, K.
coredata.dc:description: Tryptophan capped gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters (Trp-Au/Ag BNCs) based turn-off fluorescence sensor has been developed for the simple and rapid determination of histamine. Sensor parameters such as the effect of solvent, irradiation time etc. were optimized and further studies were done using these results. Fluorescence quenching of BNCs happens with the incremental addition of histamine. This might be due to the aggregation of BNCs induced by the electrostatic force of attraction between the negatively charged BNCs and amino functional groups of histamine. The sensor showed good sensitivity for the determination of histamine with a linear range of 8.5 × 10−5 M to 4.0 × 10−6 M and a limit of detection (LOD) of 9.0 × 10−7 M. Practical utility of the proposed sensor has been proved through spike recovery analysis in synthetic physiological fluids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic nanoclusters
Histamine
Turn-off fluorescence sensing
Aggregation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs, Trp-AuNCs and Trp-AgNCs towards the sensing of histamine
Effect of concentration on the fluorescence sensing of histamine
Selectivity and interference study of the proposed sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author, Swathy S. would like to acknowledge Council of Scientific and Industrial Research ( CSIR ), India for financial assistance in the form of fellowship (grant number- 09/239 (0541)/2018-EMR-I ), Sophisticated Test and Instrumentation Centre , CUSAT , Kerala for TEM and EDX analysis and CSIR- National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology , Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala for XPS analysis. The author Goldamol S. Pallam is grateful to University Grants Commission ( UGC ) for financial assistance in the form of research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–Visible spectrum of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs b) Fluorescence emission spectrum of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs c) FTIR spectra of Trp and Trp-Au/Ag BNCs.
a) XPS survey scan of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs b) XPS spectrum of Ag in Trp-Au/Ag BNCs c) XPS spectrum of Au in Trp-Au/Ag BNCs.

a) TEM image of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs b) particle size histogram of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs c) HRTEM image of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs d) HRTEM-SAED pattern of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs e) FFT image of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs showing the d spacing and corresponding plane of Au and Ag f) EDX image of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs showing the presence of Au and Ag.
Comparison of the fluorescence quenching effects of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs, Trp-AuNCs and Trp-AgNCs towards histamine.

a) Effect of solvents (0.1 M) on the fluorescence sensing of histamine using Trp-Au/Ag BNCs b) Effect of irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs in the absence (A) and presence (B) of histamine.

a) Effect of concentration of histamine on the fluorescence intensity of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs b) Linear calibration plot of concentration of histamine against I0/I (inset: linear calibration plot within the range, 2.0 × 10−5 M to 4.0 × 10−6 M). Error bars represent the standard deviation of five replicate measurements.
a) Fluorescence decay curves of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs in the absence and presence of 3.0 × 10−5 M histamine b) Overlay of emission spectrum of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs with the absorption spectrum of histamine.
a) TEM image of aggregated Trp-Au/Ag BNCs after the addition of histamine, DLS spectra of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs before (b) and after (c) the addition of histamine.
Selectivity study of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs towards histamine in presence of other species in the same class (Error bars represent the standard deviation of five replicate measurements).
Effect of structurally similar and co-existing species on the determination of histamine.
Application study of the proposed sensor in synthetic physiological fluids.

head.ce:title: Tryptophan capped gold–silver bimetallic nanoclusters-based turn-off fluorescence sensor for the determination of histamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi, 682022, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi, 682022, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tryptophan capped gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters (Trp-Au/Ag BNCs) based turn-off fluorescence sensor has been developed for the simple and rapid determination of histamine. Sensor parameters such as the effect of solvent, irradiation time etc. were optimized and further studies were done using these results. Fluorescence quenching of BNCs happens with the incremental addition of histamine. This might be due to the aggregation of BNCs induced by the electrostatic force of attraction between the negatively charged BNCs and amino functional groups of histamine. The sensor showed good sensitivity for the determination of histamine with a linear range of 8.5 × 10−5 M to 4.0 × 10−6 M and a limit of detection (LOD) of 9.0 × 10−7 M. Practical utility of the proposed sensor has been proved through spike recovery analysis in synthetic physiological fluids.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The biogenic amine, histamine (β-imidazolylethylamine), is a key mediator of various physiological processes in the human body, which is produced by the decarboxylation of l-Histidine. Histamine is produced and stored in mast cells and basophils [1]. It is highly recognized for its effect in allergic inflammatory reactions as its release leads to the activation of mast cells and basophils, resulting in the symptoms of allergy and anaphylaxis [2]. Higher level of histamine indicates Alzheimer's disease, asthma, nausea, vomiting, headache and some neuropsychiatric disorders. In addition, histamine can be found in excess in fermented food because of the enzymatic decarboxylation of histidine as a result of bacterial action [1,3]. Reports reveal that a large quantity of histamine in food such as cheese, fish and alcoholic beverages may cause poisoning. This might be due to the interaction of two receptors (H and Hz) in cell membrane of human or other animals that come into contact with histamine. The results are peripheral vascular, capillary and arterial dilation, hypotension, skin flushing, headache etc. [4]. Hence, it is necessary to quantify histamine in both food samples and body fluids.

Histamine concentration in a sample has been determined using the conventional methods such as High-Performance Liquid Chromatography, Thin-Layer Chromatography, gas chromatography, electrochemiluminescence, ultrasensitive flow injection electrochemical analysis, 1H NMR spectroscopy, Polymerase Chain Reaction Denaturing Gradient Gel Electrophoresis (PCR-DGGE), spectrophotometry, Enzyme-Linked Immuno Sorbent Assay (ELISA) and AOAC fluorometric method [3–8]. Due to the requirement of expensive instruments, tedious pre-treatment of samples, use of solvents, highly skilled operational personnel etc., real sample analysis of histamine is somewhat difficult [5,9]. A relevant tool for the analysis of biomolecules with high sensitivity is the fluorescence technique. Main advantages of employing fluorescence technique for the real sample analysis of biomolecules include high specificity, sensitivity, fast response, simplicity in the working etc. [1,9]. A few fluorescence sensors have been reported for the sensing of histamine using nanomaterials and biocomponents, but none of them involves bimetallic nanoclusters as the probe [1].

Nanomaterials are used as an emerging tool for the sensing of biomolecules. Nanomaterials replace traditional organic dyes as fluorescence probes because of certain advantages like superior optical properties, brighter fluorescence, wide excitation and emission wavelengths, excellent photostability, size-tunable fluorescence etc. [1,10,11]. Bimetallic nanoclusters (BNCs) based fluorescence sensors were reported recently for the fluorescence sensing of biomolecules. They are superior over monometallic nanoclusters (NCs) because of their improved electronic, optical and catalytic properties typically attributed to synergistic effects. Synergic effect in the case of BNCs means the combination of two types of atoms having distinct properties leading to the formation of electronically and geometrically stable NCs [12,13]. Mostly used bimetallic nanoclusters are made of Ag and Au because their size is similar to the Fermi wavelength of an electron, hence exhibit molecular-like behaviour [14]. Besides, bimetallic nanoclusters of Ag and Au have advantages like chemical stability, facile surface chemistry, excellent photostability, interesting photoluminescence properties, lower toxicity etc. In the synthesis point of view, bimetallic nanoclusters of Au and Ag are facile to be obtained due to their similar lattice constants [15–17].

Herein, we report a Trp-Au/Ag BNCs based fluorescence sensor for the determination of histamine. The main advantage of adopting tryptophan (Trp) as a capping agent instead of toxic organic stabilizing agents is that it has largest size among essential amino acids and can absorb and emit quick fluorescent radiation in the ultraviolet domain. They have good optical properties which are arising from the electronic transitions in its indole groups and its nanoscale conjugation with metal nanoparticles decreases its intrinsic fluorescence due to the enhanced life time of their excitons [18,19]. Up to our knowledge, bimetallic nanoclusters-based fluorescence sensors have not been reported yet for histamine. Fluorescence intensity of the Trp-Au/Ag BNCs quenches in presence of histamine, which is utilized in the quantitative analysis of the latter. Trp-Au/Ag BNCs showed good selectivity for histamine in presence of other structurally similar and coexisting species. Practical utility of the proposed sensor has been checked in artificial urine and blood serum and the mechanism of sensing has been explained in detail.

All the chemicals used were of analytical grade. Histamine was purchased from Acro Organics, USA, whereas Trp, tyrosine and HAuCl4 were purchased from SRL Pvt. Ltd., India. AgNO3 and histidine were purchased from Spectrochem. Pvt. Ltd., India. Dopamine, serotonin, vanillylmandelic acid, homovanillic acid and norepinephrine were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Tyramine, tryptamine and octopamine were obtained from TCI chemicals, Japan. Hi Media Laboratories provided glutamine whereas NaCl and KCl were purchased from Merck, India. Tyrosine was purchased from SRL Pvt. Ltd., India and homovanillic acid was procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA.

Fluorescence and lifetime analysis were carried out using Fluoromax-4 and Fluorolog TCSPC of Horiba Scientific, respectively. FTIR spectra were taken using JASCO-4100 FTIR whereas X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) studies were carried out using PHI 5000 Versa Probe II, ULVAC-PHI Inc., USA. UV–Visible spectra were taken using Evolution-201 UV–visible spectrophotometer and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using JEOL/JEM 2100 instrument. Particle size distributions using differential light scattering (DLS) experiment were done using Malvern Instruments of Horiba Scientific and the same was used for zeta potential analysis.

Trp-Au/Ag BNCs were synthesized according to a reported procedure [20]. 2 ml each of 10 mM of AgNO3 solution and 1 mM solution of HAuCl4 were added to 2 ml of 10−2 M Trp taken in a round bottom flask under vigorous stirring at 25 rpm. Then the flask was kept in an oil bath for 4 h at 120 °C with a stirring speed of 650 rpm. The solution turns pink colour and finally to yellow colour after forming Trp-Au/Ag BNCs. After 4 h, the solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged twice at 13,000 rpm inorder to remove any unreacted reagents. The supernatant solution containing BNCs was collected from the centrifuging tube and kept at 4 °C for further studies.

Fluorescence sensing of histamine was done by its incremental addition to a fixed volume of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs followed by measuring the quenching of fluorescence intensity of the latter. A linear calibration graph of the sensor was obtained by plotting concentration of the analyte versus I0/I, where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of BNCs in the absence and presence of histamine.

Synthesized BNCs were characterized using fluorescence, FTIR, XPS, UV–visible and EDX spectroscopic techniques. Surface charges and particle size of BNCs were measured using zeta potential, TEM, and DLS analysis.

UV–Visible spectroscopy is very useful to differentiate nanoclusters from nanoparticles because of the absence of surface plasmon peaks in the former [21,22]. In the case of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs, a broad absorption spectrum under 480 nm with no specific surface plasmon peaks was obtained, indicating the absence of the formation of large nanoparticles. It also indicates the formation of nanoclusters (Fig. 1 a) [21,22]. In the fluorescence spectrum of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs, peak with maximum emission intensity was observed at 450 nm when excited at 370 nm (Fig. 1b). So further studies were done using 370 and 450 nm as excitation and emission wavelengths.

Capping agent has an important role in the intensity and stability of BNCs [23]. Here, the proof for successful capping of Trp over Au/Ag BNCs was obtained from FTIR spectroscopy. FTIR spectra were recorded for both Trp and Trp-Au/Ag BNCs (Fig. 1c). The peak at 1411 cm−1 corresponding to COO− stretching vibration in free Trp got shifted to 1383 cm−1 after capping with Au/Ag BNCs. This might be due to the interaction of carboxyl groups of a Trp molecule with the amino groups of the adjacent Trp molecule [24]. Also, the peak at 750 cm−1, which corresponds to the N–H bending vibration in Trp is absent in Trp-Au/Ag BNCs which points to the fact that the silver ions easily bound to the electron rich N atom in Trp after the addition of AgNO3 in the reaction mixture [24]. All the above points correspond to the successful capping of Trp over Au/Ag BNCs.

XPS is a technique which is helpful to find out the valence states and binding energies of elements present in a molecule [25]. Here, XPS was used specifically to find out the presence of both Au (0) and Ag (0) in the fluorophore.

The survey scan shows the binding energy values of C1s, N1s, O1s, Ag3d and Au4f present in the BNCs (Fig. 2 a). In the spectrum of Ag, peaks showing the binding energy values 368 eV and 374 eV correspond to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 of Ag (0), which confirmed that metallic silver is present in the BNCs (Fig. 2b) [20,26]. The appearance of peaks at 84.5 eV and 88.4 eV in the spectrum of Au indicate Au (0) oxidation state in the BNCs (Fig. 2c) [15,20]. Hence the results obtained from XPS analysis confirms the presence of bimetallic nanoclusters of Au and Ag in Trp-Au/Ag BNCs solution.

Particle size analysis of the synthesized BNCs was done by conducting TEM and DLS analysis. HRTEM results show the presence of nanoclusters with an average size of 3.5 nm (Fig. 3 a). A particle size histogram representing the narrow size distribution of BNCs ranging from 1.0 to 6.0 nm is represented in Fig. 3b. HRTEM image and the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the synthesized nanoclusters were also taken inorder to find the lattice spacing and crystalline nature of BNCs (Fig. 3c and d) [27,28]. In the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) TEM image of BNCs, two different lattices with spacing 0.235 nm and 0.223 nm were found corresponding to Au and Ag nanoclusters, respectively (Fig. 3e) [24,29,30]. To support this data, EDX method was adopted inorder to confirm the presence of Trp-Au/Ag NCs (Fig. 3f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs, Trp-AuNCs and Trp-AgNCs towards the sensing of histamine
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching effects of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs, Trp-AuNCs and Trp-AgNCs by histamine were compared and is shown in Fig. 4 . It was found that histamine quenched the fluorescence intensity of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs to a maximum when compared to monometallic nanoclusters.

Fluorescence sensing of a biomolecule is highly influenced by the solvent which has been taken as the medium of analysis. Effect of solvent on the fluorescence sensing of histamine has been studied in presence of water, 0.1 M of phosphate buffer saline (PBS), citrate buffer saline (CBS), acetate buffer saline (ABS), NaOH and HCl. Histamine showed maximum quenching of fluorescence intensity of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs when water was taken as the solvent (Fig. 5 a) and hence it was optimized as the medium for the sensing of histamine with Trp-Au/Ag BNCs.

Effect of irradiation time on the fluorescence sensing of histamine was studied by comparing the change in intensity of BNCs in the absence and presence of histamine for 15 min (Fig. 5b). It was obtained that the BNCs attained stability within 10 min in the absence of histamine, whereas, it took 6 min in the presence of the analyte. So further sensing studies for histamine using Trp-Au/Ag BNCs were done by fixing the irradiation time as 6 min. Standard deviation of five replicate measurements was represented by error bars.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration on the fluorescence sensing of histamine
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimized analytical conditions, quenching of fluorescence intensity of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs at 450 nm occurred linearly with the incremental addition of histamine (Fig. 6 a). The sensor showed linear range for the determination of histamine from 8.5 × 10−5 M to 4.0 × 10−6 M. The calibration plot of I0/I against concentration of histamine follows Stern-Volmer equation [23]:
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
[ C ]
Here, [C] is the concentration of histamine whereas KSV (Stern-Volmer quenching constant) was obtained as 6.9 × 104 M−1. LOD was obtained as 9.0 × 10−7 M using the equation, 3 s/m, where s is the standard deviation of I0/I of lowest concentrations of histamine in the calibration plot and m is its slope (Fig. 6b) [31].

Basically, fluorescence quenching mechanisms involved in the case of nanoclusters for the sensing of biomolecules are static quenching, dynamic quenching and inner filter effect (IFE) [32–35]. Static quenching mechanism involves the ground state interaction between fluorophore and analyte whereas, in dynamic type, excited state interaction occurs [34]. IFE is an effect that occurs when the absorption spectrum of analyte overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorophore [34,35]. Inorder to find out whether the fluorescence quenching mechanism involved in the case of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs using histamine is static, dynamic or IFE, lifetime study of the fluorophore in the absence and presence of the analyte was done. No change in the lifetime of the BNCs was obtained after the addition of histamine (3.0 × 10−5 M), which points towards the absence of a dynamic quenching mechanism (Fig. 7 a) [31,33]. Further, the absence of any overlap of the absorption spectrum of histamine and emission spectrum of BNCs ruled out the possibility of IFE (Fig. 7b) [33].

The results obtained from TEM analysis reveal the conversion of the well dispersed BNCs into an aggregated form after the addition of histamine (Figs. 3a and 8 a). Aggregation may result in quenching of fluorescence of the nanoclusters [32,36]. Aggregation is also evident from the DLS analysis, since an increase in hydrodynamic volume of the BNCs occurred from 3.0 to 11.0 nm after the addition of histamine (Fig. 8b and c).

Zeta potential analysis was carried out inorder to understand the stability and surface effects of the synthesized BNCs [37]. Zeta potential values obtained from the analysis were −20.5 mV (BNCs) and −6.6 mV (BNCs with histamine), indicating a decrease in both the surface charge and stability of the nanoclusters [37]. This might be due to the fact that the positively charged amino functional groups of histamine make an electrostatic force of attraction with the negatively charged Trp-Au/Ag BNCs thereby reducing the surface charge of nanoclusters [38]. The previously stable BNCs with high negative charge became unstable after the addition of histamine because of the decrease in its surface charge and got aggregated [38]. All these results show the aggregation of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs due to its strong surface affinity with histamine resulted in fluorescence quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference study of the proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity study is important for a sensor as it gives an idea about how much selective the fluorophore towards the analyte species. The study was done in presence of structurally similar species, which are in the same class of histamine (its precursor and other neurotransmitters) (Fig. 9 ). Any change in the intensity of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs was not observed in the case of histidine, octopamine and glutamine whereas tryptamine and tyramine quenched the fluorescence intensity but not as much as that of histamine. This shows the greater selectivity of the proposed sensor towards histamine.

During the analysis of histamine, it is important to find out the effect of structurally similar and co-existing species on the fluorescence intensity of Trp-Au/Ag BNCs. The effect of possible interferants on the fluorescence intensity of BNCs was studied in different concentration ratios (Table 1 ). It was found that during the analysis of histamine, tyramine and tryptamine interfered even at 1:1 M ratio whereas, histidine did not interfere in the analysis even when present in 100-fold excess concentration than that of the analyte. Up to 10-fold excess concentration than that of histamine, species such as octopamine, glutamine, dopamine, vanillylmandelic acid, homovanillic acid, serotonin and norepinephrine did not interfere the analysis. At the same time, signal change produced by NaCl, KCl, Trp, tyrosine, glucose and phenylalanine were within the tolerance limit (≤5%) even when present in 100-fold excess concentration than that of histamine.

Fluorescence sensing of histamine using Trp-Au/Ag BNCs was applied in synthetic physiological fluids inorder to find its practical utility (Table 2 ). Good recovery and RSD values were obtained from the spike recovery method which confirms the successful application of the proposed sensor in body fluids. A standard method (UV–Visible spectroscopy) was used to compare the results obtained from the spike recovery method [3].

Trp-Au/Ag BNCs based turn-off fluorescence sensor has been developed for the determination of histamine. Sensing of histamine using the synthesized BNCs is supposed to be aggregation induced quenching due to electrostatic force of attraction between histamine and the fluorophore. Sensor parameters like the effect of solvent, irradiation time etc. were optimized and further studies have been done under these conditions. The sensor showed good selectivity towards histamine detection in presence of other structurally similar and co-existing biomolecules. A linear concentration range was obtained for the analysis of histamine using the synthesized BNCs from 8.5 × 10−5 M to 4.0 × 10−6 M with a detection limit of 9.0 × 10−7 M. The proposed sensor was successfully applied in synthetic urine and blood serum for the determination of histamine.
Swathy S: Investigation, formal analysis and writing original draft.; Goldamol S. Pallam: Investigation and conceptualization.; K. Girish Kumar: Conceptualization, methodology and supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author, Swathy S. would like to acknowledge Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), India for financial assistance in the form of fellowship (grant number-09/239 (0541)/2018-EMR-I), Sophisticated Test and Instrumentation Centre, CUSAT, Kerala for TEM and EDX analysis and CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala for XPS analysis. The author Goldamol S. Pallam is grateful to University Grants Commission (UGC) for financial assistance in the form of research fellowship.
